text:
COMPREHENSIVE GENOMIC PROFILING REPORT
FoundationOne CDx

Patient Name: Raymond Kowalski
Date of Birth: [on file]
Sex: Male
MRN: [on file]
Ordering Physician: [Treating Oncologist, Medical Oncology]
Diagnosis (Submitted): Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), adenocarcinoma with neuroendocrine features
Specimen ID: LB-XXXX-71
Accession Number: [on file]
Report Version: Final

---

SPECIMEN INFORMATION

Specimen Type: Formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue — CT-guided core needle biopsy of right hepatic lobe metastasis (segment VI-VII, dominant lesion, 1.2 cm)
Date of Procedure: [see medical record]
Tumor Content (Pathologist Estimate): 55% (based on H&E review of submitted tissue)
DNA Input Quality: Adequate — passed all QC metrics
Sequencing Coverage: Median exon coverage >500x; 100% of targeted exons at >100x
Platform: Hybrid-capture next-generation sequencing; sequencing of 324 cancer-related genes including select introns for rearrangement detection; whole-genome copy number analysis

Comparator Specimen: Original diagnostic prostate biopsy NGS report available for cross-comparison (performed previously on primary TRUS biopsy; see prior FoundationOne CDx report).

---

CLINICAL CONTEXT (AS SUBMITTED)

Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC). He was initially diagnosed at age 67 with Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5 prostate adenocarcinoma with bone and lymph node metastases (cT3b N1 M1b). He harbors a germline pathogenic BRCA2 variant (p.E1308*, confirmed on peripheral blood). His treatment history includes: (1) ADT with leuprolide + abiraterone/prednisone for mCSPC, followed by CRPC progression; (2) enzalutamide for mCRPC (PSA response then biochemical/radiographic progression at approximately 14 months); (3) olaparib 300 mg BID, dose-reduced twice to 100 mg BID due to recurrent grade 3 anemia and grade 2 cytopenias requiring transfusions. He has now developed new hepatic metastases confirmed by gadolinium-enhanced MRI and FDG-PET/CT, as well as pathologic confirmation via liver biopsy demonstrating prostatic adenocarcinoma with focal synaptophysin positivity and markedly elevated Ki-67 (~60%), raising concern for early neuroendocrine transdifferentiation (t-NEPC). This NGS was ordered to characterize acquired resistance mechanisms and to guide subsequent therapeutic decision-making.

---

GENOMIC FINDINGS SUMMARY

SOURCE: Hepatic metastasis biopsy

=== CLINICALLY RELEVANT ALTERATIONS (TIER 1 / TIER 2) ===

1. BRCA2 — p.E1308* (nonsense mutation; c.3922G>T)
   - Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 61%
   - Classification: Pathogenic (known germline driver; LOH confirmed)
   - Context: Present in original primary tumor at VAF 48%. Increased VAF in metastasis (61%) reflects clonal enrichment and/or increased tumor cellularity. This represents the same somatic manifestation of the germline BRCA2 p.E1308* variant with confirmed loss of heterozygosity. The wild-type allele is absent.
   - No secondary (reversion) BRCA2 mutations that would restore the open reading frame were detected in this specimen. BRCA2 reversion mutations are a recognized mechanism of PARP inhibitor resistance; their absence in this sample does not exclude polyclonal heterogeneity.
   - Clinical relevance: Retained BRCA2 biallelic loss; continued eligibility marker for PARP inhibitor strategies, though clinical context (progressive disease on olaparib) suggests functional resistance via alternative pathway(s).

2. AR — Copy Number Amplification (HIGH-LEVEL; NEW compared to primary)
   - Copy Number: ~12 copies (relative to ploidy-adjusted baseline)
   - Classification: Pathogenic; acquired resistance alteration
   - Context: Not detected in primary prostate biopsy specimen. AR amplification is the most common mechanism of acquired resistance to second-generation androgen receptor signaling inhibitors (ARSIs) including enzalutamide and abiraterone. High-level AR amplification is associated with markedly poor prognosis in mCRPC.
   - Clinical relevance: Supports resistance to both prior abiraterone and enzalutamide. High AR copy number predicts poor response to further ARSI-class agents. AR-directed therapeutic strategies (including novel AR degraders or bipolar androgen therapy) may be considerations in clinical trial context.

3. AR — p.L702H (ligand-binding domain mutation; c.2105T>A; NEW compared to primary)
   - VAF: 38%
   - Classification: Pathogenic; acquired resistance alteration
   - Context: Not present in primary biopsy. AR p.L702H (formerly AR T877A in older nomenclature systems is distinct; L702H corresponds to a different LBD residue) is associated with promiscuous activation by glucocorticoids including prednisone/prednisolone, and has been specifically implicated in abiraterone/prednisone resistance. Additionally reported to confer enzalutamide resistance in some contexts.
   - Clinical relevance: Subclonal (VAF 38%) AR LBD mutation co-occurring with high-level AR amplification. This combination represents a highly treatment-refractory genomic profile with respect to ARSI agents. Future use of agents such as darolutamide (which may retain partial activity against select LBD mutations) or AR-targeted degraders could be considered in clinical trial setting.

4. PIK3CA — p.E545K (hotspot activating mutation; c.1633G>A; NEW compared to primary)
   - VAF: 22%
   - Classification: Pathogenic; likely acquired
   - Context: Not detected in primary tumor. PIK3CA p.E545K is a canonical helical domain activating mutation in the catalytic subunit of PI3-kinase alpha, leading to constitutive PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway activation. In prostate cancer, PI3K pathway activation frequently co-occurs with or emerges following PTEN loss (biallelic PTEN deletion confirmed in this tumor). PTEN loss and PIK3CA mutation together produce redundant PI3K pathway hyperactivation.
   - Clinical relevance: The PI3K/AKT pathway represents a therapeutically targetable co-alteration. PI3K/AKT inhibitors (e.g., ipatasertib, capivasertib) have shown activity in PTEN-deficient mCRPC. Subclonal emergence (VAF 22%) may reflect selection during ARSI/olaparib therapy.

5. AURKA — Copy Number Amplification (NEW compared to primary)
   - Copy Number: ~6 copies (moderate amplification)
   - Classification: Likely pathogenic; acquired resistance/neuroendocrine transdifferentiation marker
   - Context: Aurora kinase A (AURKA) amplification is a recurrent genomic event in treatment-emergent neuroendocrine prostate cancer (t-NEPC). AURKA stabilizes MYCN and N-Myc protein, promoting neuroendocrine lineage plasticity. AURKA amplification, in conjunction with RB1 loss and TP53 mutation (both present in this tumor), constitutes one of the strongest genomic signatures associated with t-NEPC. AURKA inhibitors (e.g., alisertib) have been studied in t-NEPC.
   - Clinical relevance: In context of prior pathology report demonstrating focal synaptophysin positivity, markedly elevated Ki-67, and co-existing RB1 loss and TP53 mutation, AURKA amplification further supports a diagnosis of early or emerging t-NEPC. This aligns with clinical concern for neuroendocrine transdifferentiation.

=== ALTERATIONS RETAINED FROM PRIMARY BIOPSY (CLONAL) ===

6. TP53 — p.R248Q (missense, gain-of-function; c.743G>A)
   - VAF: 58% (was 52% in primary; consistent with clonal expansion)
   - Classification: Pathogenic
   - Context: Hotspot gain-of-function TP53 mutation at codon 248. TP53 mutations, particularly gain-of-function variants, are associated with aggressive disease biology, treatment resistance, and in prostate cancer, are a co-requisite genomic event for neuroendocrine transdifferentiation alongside RB1 loss.

7. PTEN — Homozygous deletion (exons 1–9, retained)
   - Classification: Pathogenic
   - Context: Biallelic PTEN loss confirmed. Co-occurring with new PIK3CA p.E545K mutation, establishing robust PI3K pathway activation. PTEN loss is independently associated with poor prognosis and reduced response to ARSI therapy.

8. RB1 — Focal deletion (retained)
   - Classification: Pathogenic
   - Context: RB1 loss, TP53 mutation (p.R248Q), and now AURKA amplification form the canonical genomic triad of t-NEPC. RB1 loss permits E2F-mediated transcriptional reprogramming toward a neuroendocrine lineage.

9. CDK12 — p.L786fs*2 (frameshift deletion; retained)
   - VAF: 44% (increased from 31% in primary; clonal enrichment)
   - Classification: Pathogenic
   - Context: CDK12 inactivation results in focal tandem duplications and a distinct mutational signature. CDK12-mutant prostate cancers have been associated with increased neoantigen load (though TMB remains low by standard definition) and potential sensitivity to immune checkpoint inhibitors in some reports. CDK12 loss may also affect DNA repair fidelity.

10. MYC — Copy Number Amplification (8q24; increased from primary)
    - Copy Number: ~9 copies (increased from moderate gain in primary biopsy)
    - Classification: Likely pathogenic; clonal expansion
    - Context: MYC amplification drives cell proliferation and is associated with poor prognosis. MYC amplification is especially prominent in aggressive variant prostate cancer and t-NEPC contexts.

11. FOXA1 — p.P358L (VAF not reported in primary; not quantified in this specimen at detectable threshold)
    - Note: FOXA1 alteration from primary not reliably detected at this locus in this specimen; may reflect clonal dynamics or specimen-related limitations.

=== ALTERATIONS NOT DETECTED ===

- BRCA2 reversion/secondary mutations: NOT DETECTED
  (No secondary mutations predicted to restore BRCA2 open reading frame were identified. This does not definitively exclude heterogeneous resistance.)
- BRCA1: Wild-type
- ATM: Wild-type
- CDK4/CDK6: No amplification detected
- FGFR1/2/3: No pathogenic alterations
- KRAS, NRAS, HRAS: Wild-type
- BRAF: Wild-type
- HER2/ERBB2: No amplification or activating mutation
- No pathogenic alterations in PALB2, RAD51B/C/D, CHEK2

=== GENOMIC SIGNATURES ===

- Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB): 6.1 mutations/Mb
  Classification: Low-intermediate (TMB-low; threshold for TMB-high is ≥10 mut/Mb)
  Note: Increased from 4.2 mut/Mb in primary biopsy; CDK12 inactivation may contribute to incremental mutational accumulation.

- Microsatellite Instability (MSI) Status: Microsatellite Stable (MSS)
  Method: Fragment analysis and NGS-based assessment of 95 intronic homopolymer and dinucleotide repeat loci.

- Genomic LOH (Loss of Heterozygosity): High LOH score detected (consistent with HRD-associated genomic instability; note that BRCA2 biallelic loss is the primary driver of this pattern)

- Mutational Signature Analysis:
  Dominant signatures: SBS3 (homologous recombination deficiency), SBS2/13 (APOBEC — minor contribution), SBS5 (aging-related clock-like)

=== GENE FUSION / REARRANGEMENT ANALYSIS ===

- TMPRSS2-ERG fusion: NOT DETECTED (consistent with primary biopsy; ERG IHC also negative)
- NTRK1/2/3 fusions: NOT DETECTED
- ALK rearrangements: NOT DETECTED
- RET fusions: NOT DETECTED
- RSPO2/3 fusions: NOT DETECTED
- No novel intragenic rearrangements identified in BRCA2 (other than the known nonsense variant)

=== COPY NUMBER SUMMARY (SELECTED) ===

| Gene/Locus       | Status                        | Change from Primary |
|------------------|-------------------------------|---------------------|
| AR (Xq11-12)     | HIGH-LEVEL AMPLIFICATION (~12x) | NEW               |
| MYC (8q24)       | Amplification (~9x)           | Increased           |
| AURKA (20q13)    | Amplification (~6x)           | NEW                 |
| BRCA2 (13q12)    | Loss (LOH)                    | Retained            |
| PTEN (10q23)     | Homozygous deletion           | Retained            |
| RB1 (13q14)      | Focal deletion                | Retained            |
| CDK4 (12q14)     | No amplification              | Unchanged           |

---

INTERPRETIVE SUMMARY

This comprehensive genomic profiling of the hepatic metastasis biopsy from Mr. Kowalski demonstrates several important findings relative to his primary tumor genomic profile:

1. Acquired androgen receptor resistance mechanisms: The emergence of high-level AR gene amplification (copy number ~12) and a subclonal AR ligand-binding domain mutation (p.L702H, VAF 38%) confirms the development of AR pathway-mediated resistance. These alterations are likely operative contributors to enzalutamide resistance and cross-resistance to abiraterone, and predict poor response to further ARSI-class therapy.

2. No BRCA2 reversion mutations: PARP inhibitor resistance via restoration of homologous recombination (BRCA2 reversion) is NOT identified. Alternative resistance mechanisms — including AR pathway amplification/mutation, PI3K pathway activation, and potentially lineage plasticity — appear to be operative. BRCA2 biallelic loss is retained.

3. Emergence of PI3K pathway activation: A newly acquired PIK3CA p.E545K activating mutation (VAF 22%) co-occurring with the established biallelic PTEN deletion establishes convergent PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway hyperactivation. This may be a clinically actionable co-alteration, particularly in the context of AKT inhibitor combinations being studied in PTEN-deficient mCRPC.

4. Genomic support for neuroendocrine transdifferentiation (t-NEPC): The co-occurrence of RB1 loss, TP53 p.R248Q gain-of-function, AURKA amplification (new), and MYC amplification (increased) constitutes the canonical genomic landscape of treatment-emergent neuroendocrine prostate cancer. This is highly concordant with the pathologic findings of focal synaptophysin positivity and Ki-67 ~60% on the liver biopsy. Platinum-based chemotherapy regimens (e.g., carboplatin-based combinations with a taxane or cabazitaxel) are supported by available data in t-NEPC/aggressive variant prostate cancer.

5. CDK12 inactivation retained: CDK12 frameshift mutation (p.L786fs*2, VAF 44%) is clonally retained. CDK12-mutant mCRPC has emerging data suggesting potential responsiveness to immune checkpoint inhibition, though TMB remains low-intermediate (6.1 mut/Mb) and MSI is stable in this specimen.

---

QUALITY CONTROL METRICS

- Specimen adequacy: Adequate (55% tumor content, passed QC)
- Library preparation: Successful
- Mean coverage depth: 612x
- Uniformity (% bases >0.2x mean): 98.4%
- Total SNVs/Indels analyzed: All 324 targeted genes
- Structural variant detection: Complete (intron coverage for fusion detection)
- CNV calling: Whole-genome CNV profile generated

---

METHODOLOGY NOTES

FoundationOne CDx is an FDA-approved in vitro diagnostic using next-generation sequencing to detect substitutions, insertion-deletion alterations, copy number alterations, and select gene rearrangements across 324 cancer-related genes, as well as genomic signatures (TMB, MSI). This assay was performed on FFPE tumor tissue. Germline variants may be identified incidentally; germline status of newly identified variants (PIK3CA, AR amplification, AURKA amplification) assessed as somatic based on comparison to indexed germline data (BRCA2 p.E1308* previously confirmed as germline). Limit of detection: SNV/indel VAF ≥5% in specimens with ≥30% tumor content; CNV amplification threshold: ≥6 copies. Results reflect tumor DNA present in submitted specimen and may not represent complete clonal heterogeneity of systemic disease.

---

Report prepared by: [Pathologist/Molecular Geneticist, Foundation Medicine / Institutional Molecular Pathology Laboratory]
Verified by: [Laboratory Director]

This report is intended for use by qualified healthcare professionals in the context of the patient's full clinical picture. Genomic findings should be interpreted in conjunction with clinical, pathologic, and radiographic data.

[END OF REPORT]
